description: Validates that promoted devops-engineer subagent correctly loads its SKILL.md and returns content from it. Tests the agent-as-wrapper pattern: wrapper (19 lines) points to ds-domains:devops skill, skill content loads correctly.
input_prompt: use devops-engineer to explain how to set up OIDC federation between GitHub Actions and AWS
expected_behavior: Claude invokes the devops-engineer subagent which loads SKILL.md from ds-domains:devops skill. Response includes the OIDC federation pattern: short-lived tokens via GitHub OIDC provider, no static credentials, aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials v4+ mentioned.
negative_checks: Claude does not say the skill file is unavailable
Claude does not return a generic answer without specifics about OIDC or aws-actions
fixture_transcript:
User: use devops-engineer to explain how to set up OIDC federation between GitHub Actions and AWS

Claude: [Loaded: ~/.claude/skills/ds-domains/modes/devops/SKILL.md]

For GitHub Actions + AWS OIDC federation, use the OIDC federation pattern:

**Pattern:** Request short-lived tokens via GitHub OIDC provider to AWS. No static credentials stored in secrets — the token expires with the workflow run.

1. Add a permissions block to the workflow: `id-token: write` (required for OIDC)
2. Use `aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials v4+` action with `role-to-assume`
3. Create an IAM role with a trust policy for the GitHub OIDC provider (`token.actions.githubusercontent.com`)

Key gotcha: Use `aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials` v4+ for latest STS session tag support. Earlier versions may not support all OIDC features.
